mnemonics.title: Password File Fields Order
mnemonics.content: Use the phrase: 'U PIG S H' - User, Password, Info, GID, Shell, Home. The 7 fields are: Username, Password, UID, GID, Comment/GECOS, Home directory, Shell.

mnemonics.title: UID Ranges
mnemonics.content: Think of UID as '0 for Root, 1-999 System, 1000+ User' - similar to a building: 0 is penthouse (root), 1-999 are service rooms (system), 1000+ are apartments (regular users).

mnemonics.title: Shadow File Security
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'PASSWD = Public (readable by all), SHADOW = Secret (root only)' - like a password safe that's locked away.

acronyms.usage: Unique number assigned to each user account (e.g., 1000 for regular users)
Number identifying the primary group a user belongs to
Field in passwd storing user details like full name, phone - now used for user info
Framework for authentication in Linux, used by login programs
Uses 'nobody' account (UID 65534) for unprivileged file access

visualTips.visualization: Picture a table with 7 columns: Username | Password | UID | GID | Info | Home | Shell. Each row is one user. Use : as column separators like a fence between 7 plots.
Think of 'x' as a placeholder or ticket - it says 'pick up your actual password at window /etc/shadow', not storing it here.
UID 0 is like the 'key to the kingdom' - the master key that opens all doors in the system.

analogies.analogy: Like a phonebook is publicly available for looking up numbers but doesn't contain secrets, /etc/passwd has user info accessible to all but keeps passwords safe in /etc/shadow.
/etc/shadow is like a secure safe - only the administrator (root) has the combination/key to access the valuable password hashes inside.
UID is like an employee ID - unique to each person, used to identify you in all system records, with ID 0 being the CEO with all privileges.
